children.name: Ambystoma
children.description: Stocky burrowing salamanders of North America. The axolotl is permanently neotenic — retaining larval features including spectacular external gills throughout its adult life. Its extraordinary ability to regenerate lost limbs, heart tissue, and portions of the brain makes it one of the most studied animals in regenerative biology. The wild population is critically endangered in a single lake in Mexico City.

children.name: Ambystoma mexicanum
children.description: Neotenic; retains external gills as an adult; can regenerate entire limbs and portions of its heart and brain with perfect fidelity; wild population critically endangered in Lake Xochimilco, Mexico City; millions bred in captivity worldwide.

children.name: Ambystoma tigrinum
children.description: Largest terrestrial salamander in North America at up to 33 cm; some isolated lake populations remain permanently neotenic.

children.name: Ambystoma maculatum
children.description: Photosynthetic algae live inside embryo cells — the only vertebrate with a photosynthetic endosymbiont; adults migrate to breeding ponds on the first warm rainy night of spring.

children.name: Ambystoma opacum
children.description: Breeds in autumn in dry riverbeds, guarding eggs until autumn rains fill the pool; larvae then hatch with a head start over spring-breeding competitors.

children.name: Ambystoma californiense
children.description: Endangered; dependent on California vernal pools; listed under the US Endangered Species Act.

children.name: Ambystoma jeffersonianum
children.description: A slender, long-toed mole salamander of eastern North America, breeding in late winter vernal pools; named after Jefferson College in Pennsylvania, not a person. Near Threatened due to habitat loss and hybridization with unisexual Ambystoma.

children.name: Ambystoma laterale
children.description: Characterized by vivid blue and white flecks on dark brown or black skin; ranges across northeastern North America into Canada, often hybridizing with Jefferson salamander to form unisexual polyploid populations.

children.name: Ambystoma annulatum
children.description: Endemic to the Ozark highlands of Arkansas, Missouri, and Oklahoma; breeds in autumn ponds unlike most congeners; distinguished by bold yellow or cream crossbands on a dark body.

children.name: Ambystoma talpoideum
children.description: The smallest eastern North American Ambystoma; highly fossorial and frequently neotenic in Gulf Coastal Plain populations; ranges from South Carolina west to Texas.

children.name: Ambystoma texanum
children.description: Widespread in central North America from Michigan south to Texas; breeds in late winter in temporary ponds and ditches; often found hybridizing with A. laterale in the northern part of its range.

children.name: Ambystoma barbouri
children.description: Breeds in gravelly streams rather than ponds, an unusual trait for the genus; restricted to the Interior Low Plateaus of Kentucky, Ohio, Indiana, and Tennessee. Named after herpetologist Thomas Barbour.

children.name: Ambystoma gracile
children.description: Ranges from northern California to southeastern Alaska along the Pacific Coast; produces toxic skin secretions from parotoid glands and tail base; larvae are often neotenic in high-elevation lakes.

children.name: Ambystoma macrodactylum
children.description: The most widely distributed mole salamander in western North America, ranging from coastal California to British Columbia and inland to Idaho and Montana; recognized by a yellow or tan dorsal stripe and notably elongated fourth toe.

children.name: Ambystoma cingulatum
children.description: Federally Threatened in the US; dependent on longleaf pine flatwoods of the southeastern Coastal Plain; frosted gray reticulated pattern distinguishes it from the closely related reticulated flatwoods salamander.

children.name: Ambystoma bishopi
children.description: Critically Endangered; restricted to a small area in the Florida panhandle; differentiated from A. cingulatum by molecular data and by a finer, more intricate reticulated pattern. Named after herpetologist Sherman C. Bishop.

children.name: Ambystoma mabeei
children.description: A small, secretive mole salamander of the Atlantic Coastal Plain from Virginia to South Carolina; breeds in winter ephemeral ponds within sandy pine flatwoods and mixed hardwood forests. Named after W.C. Mabee.

children.name: Ambystoma andersoni
children.description: Critically Endangered; permanently neotenic; known only from Lake Zacapu in Michoacán, Mexico; threatened by water extraction, pollution, and introduced fish. Named after biologist John Anderson.

children.name: Ambystoma dumerilii
children.description: Critically Endangered; permanently neotenic like the axolotl; endemic to Lake Pátzcuaro, Michoacán, Mexico; faces severe decline from introduced carp and tilapia, water pollution, and over-harvesting. Named after French herpetologist André Duméril.

children.name: Ambystoma ordinarium
children.description: Endangered; a small, stream-dwelling Ambystoma endemic to the Sierra Madre Occidental of Michoacán and Jalisco, Mexico; inhabits cool mountain streams and pine-oak forests above 2,000 m.

children.name: Ambystoma rivulare
children.description: Endangered; inhabits cold mountain streams in the Transvolcanic Belt of central Mexico (Michoacán and State of Mexico); sensitive to water quality and threatened by deforestation and agricultural runoff.

children.name: Ambystoma velasci
children.description: A robust mole salamander of the Mexican Plateau, ranging across central Mexico from Jalisco to Tamaulipas; closely related to the tiger salamander complex and frequently neotenic in high-altitude lakes.

children.name: Ambystoma taylori
children.description: Critically Endangered; permanently neotenic; confined to the saline-alkaline Lake Alchichica in Puebla, Mexico; threatened by water extraction for irrigation and human consumption. Named after herpetologist Edward Harrison Taylor.

children.name: Ambystoma altamirani
children.description: An Endangered stream salamander of the high-altitude pine-fir forests of the Transvolcanic Belt in central Mexico, found on the slopes of Nevado de Toluca, Popocatépetl, and surrounding mountains at elevations of 2,800–3,800 metres. Inhabits cold, fast-flowing mountain streams and their margins. Some populations exhibit facultative neoteny, with larvae retaining gills in permanent cold streams where metamorphosis offers no survival advantage. Threatened by deforestation, water extraction, and invasive trout introduced for sport fishing that prey on larvae and compete for food.

children.name: Ambystoma amblycephalum
children.description: A Vulnerable mole salamander of the Sierra de Manantlán and adjacent ranges of Jalisco and Michoacán in western Mexico, inhabiting pine-oak forests at elevations between 1,700 and 2,400 metres. Recognised by its proportionally broad, flat head relative to other Ambystoma. Little studied in the field; most known from specimens collected near temporary seasonal ponds and streams where it breeds in the rainy season. Threatened by cattle grazing, logging, and the conversion of highland forest to agriculture — pressures that have severely fragmented mole salamander habitat throughout western Mexico.

children.name: Ambystoma bombypellum
children.description: A Critically Endangered mole salamander with an extremely restricted range in the highlands of Tlaxcala and adjacent Puebla states in central Mexico, at elevations around 2,400–2,700 metres. Its species name (bombypellum, 'silky-skinned') refers to the unusually smooth texture of its skin compared to related species. One of the least studied Ambystoma, known from very few specimens; its precise breeding habitat and ecology remain poorly documented. The heavily agricultural and urbanised landscape of the Tlaxcala highlands leaves very little natural habitat remaining, and population trends are presumed severely declining.

children.name: Ambystoma flavipiperatum
children.description: A Vulnerable mole salamander of the mountains of Jalisco in western Mexico, characterised by scattered yellow or yellowish spots on a dark brown to black dorsum — a pattern reminiscent of the spotted salamander but on a separate evolutionary lineage. Inhabits pine-oak and cloud forest at elevations of 1,600–2,600 metres. Breeds in temporary ponds and slow streams during the summer rainy season. Like most western Mexican Ambystoma it is poorly known; its presence is highly localised and it appears to be genuinely uncommon across its range, not merely overlooked. Its forest habitat is subject to ongoing clearing for maize and avocado cultivation.

children.name: Ambystoma granulosum
children.description: An Endangered mole salamander of the pine-oak highlands of Jalisco and Michoacán in western Mexico, notable for the distinctly granular or rough texture of its dorsal skin — an unusual trait among Ambystoma, most of which have smoother integument. Found at elevations between 1,800 and 2,700 metres in areas with intact forest cover and seasonal ponds. Deforestation and the draining of highland wetlands for cattle ranching have eliminated much of its habitat. Very few field studies have been conducted; population size is unknown but presumed to be declining.

children.name: Ambystoma krausei
children.description: An Endangered mole salamander with a restricted range in the highlands around Guadalajara, Jalisco, and adjacent areas of central-western Mexico. Inhabits pine-oak forest at moderate to high elevations, breeding in temporary rain-filled ponds during the summer wet season. Distinguished from sympatric species by a combination of body proportions and colouration. Like the majority of Mexican highland Ambystoma it faces intense pressure from urban and agricultural expansion — the city of Guadalajara and its satellite municipalities have absorbed enormous areas of former highland amphibian habitat over the past four decades.

children.name: Ambystoma leorae
children.description: A Vulnerable mole salamander of the highlands of Jalisco and Guanajuato in central-western Mexico, with a range centred on the mountains east and south of the city of León. Found in pine-oak forest and associated seasonal wetlands at elevations between 1,800 and 2,600 metres. Typical of many Mexican Ambystoma in being secretive and fossorial outside the breeding season, making population assessment difficult. The León metropolitan area and its surrounding agricultural belt represent a major threat through direct habitat destruction and water extraction from highland aquifers that feed breeding ponds.

children.name: Ambystoma lermaense
children.description: Critically Endangered; a permanently neotenic Ambystoma known from the Lerma wetland system in the State of Mexico, one of the most imperilled lacustrine salamanders in the world. Like the axolotl and the Lake Pátzcuaro salamander it never metamorphoses, retaining its external gills and aquatic larval form throughout life. The Lerma River valley has been drastically altered — the wetlands that once surrounded the upper Lerma lakes were drained for agriculture and water supply beginning in the 1940s, and the salamander's habitat has been reduced to a tiny fraction of its former extent. Pollution, introduced fish, and continued water extraction keep the population in a precarious state.

children.name: Ambystoma rosaceum
children.description: A Vulnerable mole salamander of the Sierra Madre Occidental, ranging across the highland pine and pine-oak forests of Chihuahua, Sonora, and Sinaloa in northwestern Mexico and marginally into extreme southeastern Arizona — making it one of the northernmost Mexican Ambystoma. Its name refers to the Tarahumara (Rarámuri) people who inhabit the Sierra Madre. Breeds in mountain streams and seasonal ponds after summer rains. Its relatively large range gives it more resilience than most Mexican Ambystoma, but timber harvesting, cattle grazing, and the intensification of illegal logging in the Sierra Madre continue to degrade its habitat.

children.name: Ambystoma schmidti
children.description: Critically Endangered; known from very few localities in the high-altitude forests of the Sierra Madre Oriental in Nuevo León and Tamaulipas, northeastern Mexico, above 3,000 metres. One of the most range-restricted Ambystoma, effectively confined to the cold spruce-fir forests near the summit of Cerro Potosí — one of the highest peaks in northeastern Mexico. Named after the American herpetologist Karl Patterson Schmidt. Its tiny, fragmented habitat is threatened by cattle grazing, fuelwood cutting, and the potential encroachment of climate warming on the cold-adapted forest zone near the summit.
